I bought these along with the other brand to see if they worked better
than the homemade traps, they do very good catching them but the problem
is the holes are way too big. My problem got worse as they continued to
breed and I could not find out how I poured boiling hot water down my
drains everything was clean until last night I noticed upstair's in the
bathroom they were going in and out of the trap especially the large
center hole and there was larvae in it. I threw all traps away and am
using my diy traps and the issue is already getting better. Search diy
fruit fly traps and you will have much better luck I like to rinse out
and drill small holes and I mean small not like this companies in 2
liter bottles along the top sides and the cap and put apple cider
vinegar with a drop of dish soap and some sugar in it if you have some
fermented juice they love it too basically anything fermented you can
add to the mix. It also helps to wrap or stick sticky traps around the
traps to catch even more. Be aware they breed in house plant soil (how I
got mine but I've sense taken them outside), drains, trash cans and
keep everything clean you can also pour bleach down drains. This company
is just trying to get you to keep buying these things because you can't
reuse them they just keep breeding and you just keep buying more it's a
never ending cycle even though your killing them they just multiply. I
can't speak for the other company that sells a similar product but I
think the same thing happens with that.

The cute little red plastic apples are a great design. It's too bad they
are paired with lure which does not work.

I've placed four of the six apples in areas of breeding and
concentration. One on the table with plants, one right in front of me on
my desk where I work, one by the kitchen sink, and one on the kitchen
island next to another plant.

They have been out for a couple weeks now. And not a single fruit fly
can be seen inside any of the traps! In fact, I've already killed two
flying round me this morning with the apple trap less than two feet
away. Yesterday I found one fruit fly crawling around the window sill in
the kitchen mere inches away from the trap. It never did go in the trap!

I don't know if the lure expires or not. It does have a faint vinegary
smell. But as these were sold to me, they are absolutely worthless!

Absolutely. Turning the sarcasm dial to *unreasonable*:

---

I am currently living in an active infestation. I have no idea where
these creatures came from or how they managed to establish a thriving
civilization so quickly, having deep-cleaned my room and offering
absolutely no food, water, or emotional support to these bugs. And yet,
they have moved in and declared sovereignty.

In a panic, I bought several traps. This one now lives on my work desk,
because nothing says "professional" like taking meetings while insects
launch themselves into your face like kamikaze pilots.

The trap smells VILE, like rotting fruit and decay. I can smell it the
moment I enter the room. The bugs are clearly obsessed with the scent,
but instead of going inside the trap like advertised, they just loiter
around it like it's a sketchy bar.

After more than 24 hours, one trap caught **five** bugs. The other
caught **none**. Meanwhile, a different brand trap elsewhere has
racked up a body count, which confirms this product's only real function
is making my room smell awful.

Would not buy again unless I decide my room needs to smell like death
**and** still have bugs.

So I purchased the Terro fruit fly traps based on all the positive
reviews. They arrived promptly and was very excited to try them at our
ice cream and smoothies store. We sometimes have 1-2 fruit flies but not
very often. Either way doesn't look good, no customer wants to be
bother by little pesky flies. We keep everything in the store spotless,
but we'll still get a couple of those little visitors from time to
time. I placed 2 traps and after a week, zero got caught. I then added
the other 2 traps that came in the package. It has been a few more days
since that, and still none has fallen into the trap. They liquid in them
smells 100% like vinegar so that is the predominant smell in the room /
area where the traps are placed. Looks like I'll be trying a different
product because as I said, this one did not work for us at all.

DOES NOT WORK. I have a gnat infestation in my indoor office plants.
Because they are in my office, where I spend 8 hours per day, I needed
them gone, stat. I tried 3 different methods of gnat elimination: (1)
Terro apple-shaped fly traps; (2) Shallow dishes of liquid dish soap;
(3) Zevo plug-in flying insect trap.

After 3 days, here are the results:

\(1\) Terro apple-shaped fly traps: Easy to set up and fill with their
"special" gnat-attracting formula which I am sure is just apple cider
vinegar. The apple containers are small and cute. But, they don't work.
I CAUGHT 1 (ONE) GNAT using 4 different traps placed on top of soil in
my plant containers.

\(2\) Shallow dishes of dawn dish soap: Easy to set up and fill.
However, the liquid soap starts to dry out and harden after several
days. They are a bit of a pain to wash out and refill, icks me out, plus
I think it's a waste of dish soap. I CAUGHT 50 GNATS using this
method--I used 4 different shallow containers placed on top of soil in
my plant containers.

\(3\) Zevo plug-in flying insect trap. This plug-in fly trap was super
easy to set up. It's basically a blue light that attracts the insects
and super sticky paper (comes with replacement inserts) to trap them. I
CAUGHT 40 GNATS using this method. This trap is the furthest from my
plants--it's about 6 feet away. If I move it closer, it will probably
work even better. This is my favorite scent-free, mess-free method that
actually works.

If I didn't use the other 2 methods, I would have about 100 more gnats
still flying around my office space, ick. Save your money and don't
buy.

Two weeks of use, ZERO dead gnats. If you have fungus flies / gnats,
this product won't help. Fruit flies seem to be attracted to the Apple
cider vinegar and soap concoction, but overall not impressed for use
against "fungus flies" (*aka tiny black flying bugs that buzz
randomly around your face\...). Would not recommend for this type of
invasion. Next step will be throwing away all the house plants and
starting over.

We live around multiple mature trees and get gnats and fruit flies every
season change. We put 8 of these around our house and have had them out
for over a month. NOT A SINGLE TRAP HAS ANY BUGS IN IT! It does not
attract them or trap them as described. Waste of money!

Totally garbage item and waste of money. I might as well of just reached
In the cupboard and poured apple cider vinegar in a cup. House is
totally full of fruit flies and after days this thing has trapped one.
Will never buy again, I'll stick to using zevo spray instead that
actually kills on contact. I have it one star for the cute little apple.

Customer image

I've been using this product successfully for a couple of years. This
year was an exception. The product had its sinus-clearing vinegar smell,
but the fruit flies weren't buying it. I am not exaggerating -- I
caught a single fruit fly in a month. I'm writing this review to
encourage you to ask for a replacement product ASAP if you're not
catching anything in the first couple of days. In the past, there are
multiple fruit flies floating in the apple within the first 24 hours
and, at minimum, multiple fruit flies congregating on the surface of the
"apple." Or did they change the formula this year? Maybe I received a
bad batch? Who knows. Until next summer when the fruit flies come back.

worthless. save your money.

2\. the product doesn't work. Caught no flies in 48 hours even though I

My advice is buyer beware. The Terro Fruit Fly Traps were a complete
dud. They did not attack fruit flies and were a complete waste of my
time and money. After using them for 2 days with no results I contacted
Terro Customer Service and was told that it could take up to 7 days for
the product to work. I was also told to call them back if the product
did not work or if Amazon for any reason could not issue a refund.

When I purchased the Terro traps the return deadline was supposed to be
available until 10-1-25. Today is 9-19-25. Unfortunately, this product
did not work at all and after contacting Terro again as instructed by
the previous representative they will not issue a refund or completely
replace the defective product. If I could I would give these bait traps
zero stars.

Would Rate a zero if I could. Do not waste money on this product. Does
not work. Did not catch a single gnat. I installed Zevo Plug-in Insect
Traps instead and very quickly have captured thousands of gnats around
my home. Apparently this Terro product is not returnable or refundable.

The squirt bottle insert worked a lot better than the plastic slider

This product did NOT work. I never felt so compelled to write reviews
before and it's not even too expensive in my opinion (though I do plan
on pursuing a refund because of how useless it is.) I felt so frustrated
with the problem of fruit flies suddenly appearing in my apartment for
the first time ever. I was looking forward for this to do the trick. It
didn't. The flies were definitely attracted to the smell of the apple
cider vinegar solution. They would sit on TOP of the apple but not go
inside. Not a single one got trapped. I felt SO let down. I felt my Zevo
plug-in to be more effective at luring fruit flies in. Albeit it is
taking longer than expected. With the average lifespan of a fruit fly
being 40-50 days, I hate the fact that I might need to wait 30 more days
for all of them to die. I just don't want to keep being let down by
products as this one has. Super bummed.

Claims each trap lasts 45 days, so a total of 270 days of coverage if
you only use 1 trap at a time.

My traps were delivered and set up August 1st and, as of writing this,
all liquid in the traps has evaporated. 18 days is all I got out of them
and that is likely a stretch as I hadn't checked them in a week or so.

The traps do look better than just leaving out little cups of apple
cider vinegar but perform horribly.

Don't waste your money unless you just want nicer looking traps that
you can fill with your own ACV mixture.

catcher, the only problem is that it doesn't catch any flies. There's
not a single fruit fly in the container and although they seem to be
hovering around the area---I've kept this catcher out for about half of
the week and absolutely nothing. Save yourself some dollars and make a
catcher yourself with sugar and apple cider vinegar.

days. 180 days? Nope.

hours and it was quite annoying.

Lately, we've been buying more fruit than usual. Something we brought
home must have had a few fruit flies or eggs, so we've got some of the
pesky critters flying around in the kitchen. It's not a big
infestation, but I do see maybe a half dozen of them a day. So, I've
tried a couple of things.

I made a couple of do-it-yourself traps. There are a couple of them on
the internet. It's apple cider vinegar in a mason jar with small holes
in the lid (or a cup with plastic wrap with holes). I made both, and
caught a few flies.

Then I bought the Terro traps. They are cute and inexpensive. I'm
pretty sure the magic "attractant" is apple cider vinegar with some
kind of surfactant to make sure the critters don't float when they hit
the liquid. I'm guessing a drop of dish wash detergent would work for
that purpose. These also caught a few flies. On several occasions, I've
seen them land on the lid and walk around for a while and then fly away.
I don't know why they don't go inside the holes - one of life's great
mysteries.

Then I bought a trap with blue light and sticky paper. This also caught
a few.

The most effective (and most fun) is to use our Dyson stick vacuum. We
have white cabinets, so it's easy to see the files when they land.
I've observed that they tend to like vertical surfaces and generally
orient themselves head-up. They also take off backwards. So, if you get
the vacuum running on high and sneak up on them from below, you can
generally suck them in. At least for me, this has some entertainment
value. My wife tells me that I remind her of Bill Murray in Ghostbusters
with that thing. But it definitely does work. Who you gonna call\...?

Bottom line\... I haven't found any magic solution to the problem.
Everything seems to work about equally well. I have my homemade traps,
the Terro traps and the light trap all deployed. There is some value in
purchasing the Terro traps, because they are the most attractive option.
They are also inexpensive. They look like little apples. Don't get hung
up on their "attractant". Apple cider vinegar and dishwasher detergent
should be an effective substitute.

Happy hunting!

reviews.review: Absolutely. Turning the sarcasm dial to *unreasonable*:---I am currently living in an active infestation. I have no idea where these creatures came from or how they managed to establish a thriving civilization so quickly, having deep-cleaned my room and offering absolutely no food, water, or emotional support to these bugs. And yet, they have moved in and declared sovereignty.In a panic, I bought several traps. This one now lives on my work desk, because nothing says “professional” like taking meetings while insects launch themselves into your face like kamikaze pilots.The trap smells VILE, like rotting fruit and decay. I can smell it the moment I enter the room. We went from having a dozen to dozens of dozens. Our kitchen is crawling with them. The traps allow the fruit flies to move in and out freely so the flies that don't die just reproduce more resistant flies.How did I end up fixing this Terro created problem? By making my own fruit fly traps with jars filled with red wine, plastic wrap over the top with a rubber band to hold it in place, and then poking holes through them. I added the Balsamic vinegar to the Terro solution and left the house to run some errands. When I returned just an hour later, one of the traps already had 10 flies in it. By last night (24 hours later) there were almost 20 in one of them and 10 in another. I'm hoping that by tonight our bizarre infestation will be almost eradicated. But it's the BALSAMIC VINEGAR that I added that made it effective. I don't regret my purchase because these containers were worth the initial investment. My brother said that these used to have a pheromone in them to attract fruit flies but apparently from the reviews, they changed the formula over the past couple of years, and there was nothing in the ingredient list besides the detergent and vinegar.

reviews.review: Works well i just think it’s slightly over priced for what it actually is.. basically a vinegar trap just in a cuter display. I set out only two of the four in my kitchen and the next morning i didn’t notice anymore fruit flies. I say these work best if you set them out the moment you see the pests have arrived. Get rid of them quickly.The more you have flying around, the harder it’ll be to rid your home of them for a few days. Anyone can use these. Extremely easy to set up.. everything is ready to go. Give the solution a little shake and pour a little bit into the plastic apples and place them around your home where your experiencing these flies..After that, they work by themselves.. the flies are attracted to the vinegar, the fly into the liquid and down. reviews.review: Does it work ? Yes, but takes a few days to start catching them.Does it catch them all ? AlmostThe liquid lure made them come a running, but took them a few hours to go in the holes of the trap.Trick below to make the liquid lure provided with this product last longer blow.Pop the trap containers lid off that's provided with this kit, dump some honey 🍯 in first, but only enough to cover the bottom of the container floor.Then snap the traps lid cover back on, now use the lure solution provided, but just squeeze enough out to just cover the top of the honey, doesn't need to be deep.Bingo ready...Making more traps .I also took a woman's shaving gel cap lid and made more traps.The shaving cap lid did just as well as the small catch containers provided with this product.Simply I was able to make more traps and make the lure solution provided in the kit last way way longer saving $$$$.Note, I did try to make the home version of this trap with apple cider vinegar and dish soap, tried two different soaps, and even added sugar and the fruit flies ignored the home made version of this trap.But the honey and liquid lure on top snagged them.

reviews.review: If you are looking to murder fruit flies with little effort, this is the tool.Fruit flies lied to me, stole my children, hacked into my bank account to deplete me of resources and bribed a senator to invalidate my citizenship in my home country. When I set out for revenge, I knew there were many fruit flies in the world, but I had no idea how many. They are an uncountable force, and they are taking every opportunity to invade your home.When I set out the magic apple it finally allowed me to capture and interrogate a fruit fly I had in a headlock about the whereabouts of my children, and by the the time this occurred there were already 10-20-30, nay, legion of fruit flies that were already deposed.If you are living a life on the edge, seeking your family and have discovered that fruit flies are responsible, then there is no other option. Just know: fruit flies work for other bugs, and those bugs do not have the same weaknesses, and do not respond the same to interrogation. While a headlock worked on fruit flies to gather leads about my children, these larger bugs may require a Brazilian Jiu Jitsu triangle to submit.Again, if you are like me and protecting or searching for your children, you must use the most extreme option, and this is what has helped me in my journey so far.

reviews.review: Works really well to get rid of a full scale fruit fly invasion!I really hate fruit flies, and they can be very hard to get rid of if you get a big invasion.  I had a mishap where a banana fell behind something and I didn't realize it until a fruit fly population exploded out of that overripe banana.  I had one of those glue board traps where a UV light and a fan suck the flies in, but that only captured so many per day, not enough to quell the invasion.  I was overrun with them because they weren't all getting captured and were multiplying like mad.So I tried this chemical trap, and it immediately attracted them and started getting rid of hordes of them every day.  My only suggestion is that the liquid used has a weird vinegary and sweetish smell like rotting fruit, but I guess that's the whole point to attract them since they're fruit eaters.  I put it in a corner away from where I am so I don't smell it, but the flies go straight for it.I've given up bananas until I'm sure these nasty little critters are long gone.  They hijack in on bananas, so from now on if I see even one fly, this trap is coming out again!

reviews.review: These work amazingly well. I made a mistake and went camping for the weekend. I usually remember to remove perishables. But this time we forgot a couple bananas in the fruitbowl.
